title: 10 best herbal decoctions for breastfeeding breast pain
intro_desc: Herbal decoctions for breastfeeding breast pain are a natural remedy that combines herbs to create a soothing liquid extract, relieving discomfort and inflammation associated with engorgement, plugged ducts, and nipple soreness during lactation. These decoctions help by reducing swelling, alleviating pain, and promoting milk flow, making it easier for new mothers to continue breastfeeding. Examples of effective herbal decoctions include those made with fenugreek, blessed thistle, and comfrey, which have been shown to improve symptoms and increase confidence in nursing. By providing relief from breast pain, these decoctions enable mothers to bond with their babies and enjoy the many benefits of exclusive breastfeeding.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Cleavers decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because of its potent anti-inflammatory properties, which soothe and calm the tender breast tissue. The decoction's warming effect also helps to increase blood flow and reduce swelling, making it easier for milk ducts to open and for babies to latch on. Additionally, Cleavers' antispasmodic qualities help to relax and ease cramping and spasms in the breasts, allowing mothers to nurse their little ones with greater comfort and confidence.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Cleavers' anti-inflammatory compounds help reduce swelling and ease discomfort associated with engorgement, mastitis, or nipple soreness during breastfeeding.

Antibiotic and antifungal properties: The antimicrobial properties of Cleavers decoction can help combat bacterial and fungal infections that may be contributing to breast pain or nipple thrush, promoting a healthy nursing environment.

Lymphatic stimulating properties: Cleavers' ability to stimulate lymphatic flow helps remove excess milk and reduce swelling, alleviating breast congestion and discomfort that can make breastfeeding painful or uncomfortable.
remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: These plant compounds help reduce inflammation and alleviate pain in the breasts, making it easier for breastfeeding women to nurse their babies.
Phenolic acids: Phenolic acids in Galium aparine decoctions have anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce breast engorgement and alleviate pain associated with breastfeeding.

Saponins: Saponins in Galium aparine have a mild antispasmodic effect, which can help relax the muscles in the breast and surrounding areas, reducing pain and discomfort associated with breastfeeding.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are used for their antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties to soothe breast pain and discomfort.
Stems: The stems are used for their ability to reduce inflammation and ease breast engorgement, making them a popular choice for breastfeeding mothers.
Flowers: The flowers of Galium aparine are used for their sedative and antispasmodic effects to calm breast tenderness and promote relaxation.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1-2 tablespoons of dried galium aparine leaves and stems or 2-3 tablespoons of fresh material for decoction.
Combine the galium aparine material with 8-10 cups of water in a saucepan and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat to a simmer and let the mixture steep for 5-10 minutes or until the liquid has reduced slightly.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container to remove the solids.
Store the cooled decoction in the refrigerator for up to 2 days or freeze for later use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Stinging nettle decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties, which soothe and calm the breast tissue. Nettle's ability to reduce swelling and alleviate discomfort makes it an effective remedy for engorgement and nipple soreness often experienced by new mothers during the early days of breastfeeding. As nettle's healing compounds are absorbed through the skin, they ease tension and promote a sense of relief, allowing women to focus on nurturing their newborns with comfort and confidence.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Stinging nettle's anti-inflammatory properties help reduce swelling and discomfort in the breasts, making it a natural remedy for alleviating breastfeeding-related engorgement and pain.
Antioxidants: The antioxidants present in stinging nettle decoctions help protect the breast tissue from oxidative stress and damage, which can contribute to breast pain during breastfeeding.

Uterotonic properties: Stinging nettle's uterotonic effects can help improve milk flow and reduce breast congestion, thereby relieving pressure and discomfort that can lead to breast pain in lactating women.
remedy_constituents: Alkaloids: Alkaloids present in Urtica dioica, such as berberine and theobromine, help with breastfeeding breast pain by reducing inflammation and relaxing muscle spasms, which can contribute to breast tenderness.

Phenolic acids: Phenolic acids, including caffeic acid and chlorogenic acid, possess anti-inflammatory properties, which help alleviate breast pain associated with breastfeeding by reducing inflammation and promoting blood flow to the affected area.

Triterpenoids: Triterpenoids, like ursolic acid, have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help alleviate breast pain by reducing inflammation, promoting tissue repair, and protecting breast tissue from oxidative damage.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are used due to their rich content of salicylic acid, which has anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe breast pain.
Roots: They are used for their ability to balance hormonal changes and reduce inflammation, making them a common choice for relieving breast discomfort.
Stems: They are used due to their high concentration of antioxidants and flavonoids, which help reduce inflammation and ease breast pain.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 25-50 grams of urtica dioica leaves and stems from a clean and shaded area.
Wash the harvested urtica dioica in cold water to remove dirt and debris thoroughly.
Chop the washed urtica dioica into small pieces to release their bioactive compounds quickly.
Steep one part of chopped urtica dioica in four parts of boiling water for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Oats decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because it provides a soothing and calming effect on the breasts. The anti-inflammatory properties of oats help to reduce swelling and discomfort, while its natural emollients moisturize and soften the nipples, making them less prone to cracking and irritation. Additionally, the gentle warmth from the decoction can ease any tension or tightness in the breast tissue, promoting a more comfortable and relaxed nursing experience.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory property: Oats decoction has anti-inflammatory compounds that reduce swelling and ease breast tenderness, making it more comfortable for breastfeeding mothers.

Antioxidant property: Oats contain antioxidants that neutralize free radicals, which can cause oxidative stress and contribute to breast pain, thereby promoting overall breast health during lactation.

Soothing and emollient property: The mucilages present in oats decoction provide a soothing, cooling sensation on the nipples and breasts, reducing discomfort and making it easier for new mothers to breastfeed.
remedy_constituents: Fatty acids: Fatty acids, particularly oleic acid and palmitic acid, found in Avena sativa have anti-inflammatory properties, which help reduce breast pain and inflammation associated with breastfeeding.

Avenanthramides: Avenanthramides are unique phenolic compounds found in Avena sativa. They have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help alleviate breast pain and discomfort in breastfeeding women.

Sterols: Sterols, particularly beta-sitosterol, found in Avena sativa have been reported to have anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, which can help reduce breast pain and discomfort associated with breastfeeding.
remedy_parts: Roots: Avena sativa roots are used to make decoctions for breastfeeding breast pain due to their high saponin content, which helps to reduce inflammation and alleviate pain.
Leaves: Avena sativa leaves are used to make decoctions for breastfeeding breast pain due to their antioxidant properties, which help to reduce oxidative stress and alleviate pain.
Seeds: Avena sativa seeds are used to make decoctions for breastfeeding breast pain due to their high fatty acid content, which helps to reduce inflammation and alleviate pain.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1-2 cups of dried avena sativa root or 2-3 cups of fresh root.
Clean the harvested avena sativa root by rinsing it with cold running water.
Chop the cleaned avena sativa root into small pieces to increase surface area.
Combine the chopped avena sativa root with 2 cups of water in a saucepan.
Simmer the mixture for 10-15 minutes at a low heat over a double boiler.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Dandelion decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because of its unique properties that alleviate discomfort. The herb's anti-inflammatory compounds reduce swelling and ease tension in the breast tissue, providing relief from engorgement and nipple soreness. Additionally, dandelion's natural diuretic qualities help to expel excess milk, further reducing pain and discomfort. By promoting healthy circulation and lymphatic flow, dandelion decoctions also help to clear out toxins and waste products that can contribute to breast pain during breastfeeding.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Dandelion's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as sesquiterpenes and flavonoids, help reduce swelling and inflammation in the breasts, providing relief from engorgement and soreness.

Antioxidant properties: Dandelion's antioxidant agents, including vitamins A, C, and E, protect breast tissue from oxidative stress and damage, which can contribute to pain and discomfort during breastfeeding.

Laxative properties: Dandelion roots and leaves have a natural diuretic effect, helping to reduce water retention and alleviate breast congestion, which is a common cause of breast pain in breastfeeding mothers.
remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: These plant-derived compounds help alleviate breast pain in breastfeeding women by reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, which can ease discomfort and promote milk letdown.

Phenolic acids: These antioxidants inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes, thereby reducing breast inflammation and pain associated with breastfeeding, allowing for more comfortable nursing sessions.

Terpenoids (specifically, sesquiterpenoids): These compounds exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help mitigate breast pain and discomfort in breastfeeding women by reducing inflammation and promoting tissue repair.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They are commonly used due to their anti-inflammatory properties which help alleviate breast pain.
Roots: Roots are used for their medicinal properties, including reducing swelling and promoting healing.
Flowers: The flowers of Taraxacum officinale are used to reduce breast pain due to their ability to act as a natural anti-inflammatory.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 30 to 60 grams of fresh taraxacum officinale roots and clean them thoroughly with water.
Chop the cleaned taraxacum officinale roots into small pieces to release their active compounds.
Combine the chopped taraxacum officinale roots with 2 liters of boiling water in a saucepan.
Reduce heat to a simmer and let the taraxacum officinale roots decoct in water for 30 minutes.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve to separate the liquid from solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Purple coneflower decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because it has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce swelling and ease discomfort. The decoction's natural compounds, such as kaempferol and quercetin, work to soothe and calm the breast tissue, providing relief from engorgement and soreness. Additionally, purple coneflower's antimicrobial properties can help to combat any underlying infections that may be contributing to the pain. By reducing inflammation and promoting healthy tissue, this herbal decoction can help alleviate breastfeeding-related breast pain, allowing mothers to continue feeding their babies with comfort and confidence.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Purple coneflower decoctions have been shown to reduce inflammation in the mammary glands, which can help alleviate breast pain and discomfort associated with breastfeeding.

Antispasmodic properties: The antispasmodic compounds present in purple coneflower decoctions can help relax the muscles of the breast and nipple area, reducing spasms and contractions that can cause breast pain and discomfort.

Galactagogue properties: Purple coneflower is also considered a galactagogue herb, meaning it has been traditionally used to increase milk supply and promote lactation, which can help alleviate breast engorgement and associated pain.
remedy_constituents: Icariin: A flavonoid glycoside, icariin may help alleviate breastfeeding breast pain by reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, thereby decreasing discomfort and tension in the breast tissue.

Caffeic acid: A phenolic acid, caffeic acid may help with breastfeeding breast pain by its anti-inflammatory properties, which can reduce swelling and pain in the breast, and its antioxidant effects, which can help protect breast tissue from oxidative stress.

Cichoric acid: A phenolic acid, cichoric acid may help with breastfeeding breast pain by reducing inflammation and modulating the immune response, which can help alleviate breast pain and discomfort associated with breastfeeding.
remedy_parts: Roots: They are used for their anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties, which help alleviate breast discomfort.
Leaves: They contain compounds that help reduce inflammation and ease breast pain associated with breastfeeding.
Flowers: They are used for their mild anti-inflammatory and soothing properties, which can help calm breast tenderness.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2-4 ounces of dried echinacea purpurea roots and flowers from a trusted herbal supplier.
Combine the dried herbs with 1 quart of boiling water in a large glass pot.
Steep the mixture for 5-10 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse into the water.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container.
Store the cooled decoction in the refrigerator for up to 3 days or freeze it for later use.

The anti-inflammatory properties of hawthorn help to soothe engorged breasts, providing relief from discomfort and tenderness. remedy_parts: Fruits: Used to make decoctions for breastfeeding breast pain due to their alleged anti-inflammatory and galactagogic properties.
Leaves: Used to make decoctions for breastfeeding breast pain due to their purported anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving effects.
Barks: Used to make decoctions for breastfeeding breast pain due to their supposed ability to reduce inflammation and promote milk production.
remedy_recipe: Gather 50-100g of dried crataegus monogyna leaves and flowers.
Crush the dried herb into fine powder using a mortar and pestle.
Combine the powder with 500ml of boiling water in a saucepan.
Simmer the mixture for 10-15 minutes at low heat, then strain it.
Store the decoction in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours before consumption.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Horehound decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because it has a natural anti-inflammatory property that soothes and calms the breast tissue, reducing discomfort and tenderness. The herbal remedy also contains glycosides, which help to relax the muscles and alleviate engorgement, making it easier for milk to flow freely and relieve congestion. By using horehound decoctions, breastfeeding mothers can experience relief from nipple soreness, plugged ducts, and other common issues associated with breast pain during lactation.

Tannins: Tannins in Marrubium vulgare have astringent properties, which can help reduce milk stasis and engorgement, common causes of breast pain during breastfeeding.

Stems: Used to provide relief from engorgement and breast discomfort.
Barks: Used to reduce breast pain and inflammation due to their bioactive compounds.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 grams of dried marrubium vulgare leaves and stems in the summer months when they are in full bloom.
Crush the dried material into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle to release its active compounds.
Combine 2 teaspoons of the powdered marrubium vulgare with 1 liter of boiling water in a heat-resistant container.
Steep the mixture for 10-15 minutes in a covered container to allow the herbal properties to infuse into the water.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean container to remove the solids.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried calendula officinalis flowers at dawn or afternoon when they are most potent.

Combine the measured flowers with 1 cup of boiling water in a clean glass container.
Steep the mixture for 10 to 15 minutes or until the liquid has cooled to room temperature.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean glass bottle.

remedy_recipe: Harvest the plantago major leaves in the morning after the dew has dried and before the heat of the sun.
Dry the harvested leaves in a well-ventilated area for 2-3 weeks to reduce moisture content to 10%.
Measure out 1-2 tablespoons of dried plantago major leaves for every 250ml of water for decoction.
Bring 250ml of water to a boil in a saucepan then add the measured dried plantago major leaves and steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container and discard the solids.

remedy_recipe: Measure 25 grams of dried lavandula angustifolia flowers and place them in a heat-resistant glass container.
Combine the dried flowers with 500 milliliters of water in the glass container.
Bring the water to a boil over medium heat and then reduce the heat to low.
Simmer the mixture for 10 to 15 minutes or until the liquid has reduced slightly.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St John's wort decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because of its analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties. remedy_recipe: Harvest one cup of fresh or dried hypericum perforatum leaves and flowers for the decoction preparation process.
Combine the harvested herb material with two cups of boiling water in a heat-resistant glass container.
Steep the mixture for 10-15 minutes allowing the herbal compounds to infuse into the liquid.

remedy_recipe: Gather dried artemisia absinthium leaves and clean glass jar with lid.
Measure 20-30 grams of dried artemisia absinthium leaves and place them in the jar.
Pour boiling water over the leaves and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean container.
Store the decoction in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours before use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chamomile decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because it soothes and calms the mammary glands, reducing inflammation and discomfort. The anti-inflammatory properties of chamomile work to ease engorgement and swelling, while its calming effects on the nervous system help alleviate tension and stress that can contribute to breast pain. By promoting relaxation and reducing anxiety, chamomile decoctions provide a natural remedy for breastfeeding mothers seeking relief from breast pain and discomfort.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory property: Chamomile's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as apigenin and luteolin, can help reduce inflammation and swelling in the breasts, alleviating discomfort and pain associated with breastfeeding.

Muscle relaxant property: The flavonoids present in chamomile, like apigenin, can help relax the muscles in the breast and nipple area, reducing tension and spasms that may be contributing to pain during breastfeeding.

Antispasmodic property: Chamomile's antispasmodic properties can help soothe and calm cramping and spasms in the breasts, uterus, and other areas, providing relief from menstrual cramps, nipple soreness, and breast pain associated with breastfeeding.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of chamomile have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in reducing swelling and discomfort associated with breastfeeding-related breast pain.
Flowers: Chamomile flowers contain apigenin, an flavonoid that has a soothing effect on the breast tissue, helping to alleviate pain and discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Gather 2 teaspoons of dried matricaria chamomilla flowers and place them in a clean glass jar.
Add 8 ounces of boiling water to the jar and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into another container discarding the solids.
Store the decoction in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours before consumption or freeze for later use.
Take 1/2 cup of the cooled decoction twice daily as needed and refrigerate or freeze any leftovers.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because it has a natural ability to ease spasms and relax the uterine muscles, which can become tight and constricted during breastfeeding. This relaxation also helps to reduce inflammation and discomfort in the breasts, allowing for smoother milk flow and reduced tension. Anti-inflammatory property: Lemon balm's anti-inflammatory compounds can help reduce swelling and pain in the breasts, making it a natural remedy for mastitis or sore nipples.

Antioxidant and free radical scavenging property: Lemon balm decoctions may help neutralize free radicals and oxidative stress that can contribute to breast pain, inflammation, and damage during lactation.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, the roots help reduce inflammation and alleviate discomfort associated with breastfeeding-related breast pain.
Leaves: With their high concentration of essential oils, leaves provide a gentle, yet effective analgesic relief for breastfeeding-related nipple soreness and pain.
remedy_recipe: Gather one teaspoon of dried melissa officinalis leaves and flowers for every 250 milliliters of boiling water needed.
Combine the ingredients in a heat-resistant cup or teapot and pour in the boiling water.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes to allow the herb's active compounds to infuse into the liquid.
Strain the decoction through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean container discarding the solids.
Store the cooled decoction in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to three days for later use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chinese date decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because they promote relaxation and relief from inflammation. The natural anti-inflammatory properties of herbs such as licorice root, ginger, and dandelion root help to soothe sore nipples and reduce swelling. remedy_properties: Warming and nourishing property: Chinese dates (Da Zao) warm the breasts and nourish the milk-producing glands, alleviating breast engorgement and pain caused by poor milk letdown or low milk supply.

Soothing and calming property: The sweet and bitter flavors of Chinese dates decoctions can calm the nervous system and reduce emotional stress, which are common triggers for breastfeeding-related breast pain and discomfort.

Regulating lactation property: Chinese dates decoctions contain compounds that regulate milk production and flow, helping to relieve breast fullness, tenderness, and pain associated with oversupply or inadequate milk removal.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, which help to alleviate breast pain and discomfort associated with lactation.
Rhyzomes: Containing a high concentration of sapogenins, which have been shown to reduce inflammation and relieve breast tenderness.
remedy_recipe: Rinse one tablespoon of dried ziziphus jujuba fruit with filtered water to remove any impurities.
Soak the rinsed ziziphus jujuba fruit in 250 milliliters of boiling water for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean glass container discarding the solids.
Store the filtered decoction in an airtight container in the refrigerator and use within 24 hours, warming it before consumption as needed.
Consume one cup of the warmed ziziphus jujuba decoction twice daily for up to six weeks or as directed by a healthcare provider.

remedy_recipe: Measure 20 grams of dried vitis vinifera flowers into a heat-resistant container.
Boil 500 milliliters of water in a separate pot for 10 minutes before adding the herbs.
Add the vitis vinifera flowers to the boiling water and reduce heat to simmer for 15 minutes.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean glass container, discarding solids.
Allow the decoction to cool at room temperature before refrigerating it for up to 24 hours.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh rosa rugosa petals and leaves and clean them thoroughly.
Combine 2 tablespoons of dried rosa rugosa flowers with 8 cups of water in a pot.
Bring the mixture to a boil then reduce heat and simmer for 15 minutes.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a large bowl.
Store the cooled decoction in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 48 hours.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to breastfeeding breast pain that are treated with herbal decoctions are mastitis, fibroadenoma, and lacto-ovenal congestion. These conditions cause discomfort, swelling, and inflammation in the breasts, often accompanied by fever, chills, and nipple tenderness. Herbal decoctions made from plants like Turmeric, Ginger, and Echinacea have anti-inflammatory properties that help alleviate symptoms and promote healing. Decoctions may also be used to soothe engorged breasts, reduce swelling, and ease discomfort associated with menstruation or hormone changes.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal decoctions that help with breastfeeding breast pain is a blend of fenugreek, blessed thistle, and red clover. Fenugreek is known for its ability to increase milk supply, while blessed thistle helps to reduce inflammation and promote healing. Red clover, rich in isoflavones, supports the health of breast tissue and promotes lactation. This combination can be made into a decoction by steeping 1 teaspoon of each herb in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-10 minutes, then straining and drinking 2-3 times a day.

remedies.remedy_desc: Urtica dioica decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because it has anti-inflammatory properties that soothe and calm the breast tissue. remedy_recipe: Harvest a handful of fresh or dried urtica dioica leaves and stems in the early morning.
Rinse the harvested urtica dioica material with cold water to remove any dirt or debris.
Combine 1 cup of urtica dioica with 4 cups of water in a saucepan and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer the decoction for 5 to 10 minutes then let it steep for 20 minutes.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container and discard solids.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because it contains compounds that have anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidant properties. remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 part of dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots and clean them thoroughly to remove any debris.
Boil 1 liter of water and add 5-10 grams of the dried roots for 5 minutes.
Reduce the heat to low and simmer for an additional 15-20 minutes or until the decoction thickens.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container.
Allow the decoction to cool completely before using it as a medicinal liquid.

remedy_recipe: Gather 2-4 grams of dried cinchona officinalis bark in a clean glass container for herbal preparation.
Boil 250 milliliters of water in a saucepan on a medium heat for 5 minutes.
Add the dried cinchona officinalis bark to the boiling water and reduce heat to low simmer.

remedy_recipe: Gather 2-4 teaspoons of dried rhizome and root material of cimicifuga racemosa from a reputable supplier.
Measure out 2 cups of water and bring it to a boil in a large saucepan.
Add the dried cimicifuga racemosa material to the boiling water and stir gently to combine.
Reduce heat to a simmer and let the mixture steep for 3-5 minutes or until the desired strength is reached.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean container to discard the solids.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 10-20 dried calendula flowers and rinse them with clean water to remove any impurities.

Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or fine mesh to separate the liquid from the solids.

Use 1-2 tablespoons of the decoction as needed to soothe skin irritations and promote wound healing.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 kg of dried crataegus monogyna berries to use in decoction preparation.
Crush the dried berries into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle for 10 minutes.
Combine 1 tablespoon of the powdered berries with 1 liter of cold water in a saucepan.
Bring the mixture to a boil and then reduce the heat to a simmer for 15 minutes.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth and discard the solids to obtain the final product.

Combine the dried flowers with the measured water in a saucepan and bring to a boil.
Reduce the heat and allow the decoction to simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve and discard the solids.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 1 part of dried avena sativa root in the morning or late afternoon when the plant is dormant.
Chop the dried root into small pieces and store it in an airtight container to preserve freshness.
Combine 1 teaspoon of the chopped root with 1 cup of boiling water in a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Steep the mixture for 5-10 minutes or until the liquid has cooled down to a comfortable temperature.
Strain the liquid and discard the solids then drink the decoction immediately to ensure maximum potency.

remedies.remedy_desc: Vitex agnus-castus decoctions helps with breastfeeding breast pain because they are believed to regulate hormonal imbalances associated with lactation. remedy_recipe: Harvest 30-60 grams of dried vitex agnus-castus berries from a trusted source in late summer.
Grind the dried berries into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle for 2 minutes.
Combine 2-3 grams of the powder with 1 liter of cold water in a saucepan.
Bring the mixture to a boil then reduce heat and simmer for 30-45 minutes.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean container.

remedy_recipe: Gather 20-30 grams of dried root of althaea officinalis from a reputable supplier.
Measure out 1 quart of cold water in a large pot for the decoction.
Add the dried root of althaea officinalis to the cold water in the large pot.
Boil the mixture for 10-15 minutes or until the root material is tender and the liquid has reduced slightly.
Strain the decoction through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean container.
